text: When delegating a task effectively, always include: {{c1::the 'what', the 'why it matters', the deadline, and the level of autonomy granted}}.
explanation: Incomplete delegation is one of the most common manager failures. Research from Gallup shows that employees who receive clear delegation with context (why) perform 3x better than those given only tasks. The 'autonomy level' — do they decide independently or check in first? — prevents unnecessary escalations.
usage:
• "I need you to lead the vendor review — this is blocking our Q3 roadmap. Deadline is Thursday. You have full autonomy to make the shortlist recommendation; just loop me in before the final call."
  • "Can you own the incident postmortem? I need a first draft by Friday. Make the structural call yourself — I'll review before we share it with the team."
spanish: Al delegar eficazmente siempre incluye: el 'qué', el 'por qué importa', el plazo y el nivel de autonomía concedido. La delegación incompleta es uno de los errores más comunes de los managers.

text: To set the autonomy level when delegating, use one of three phrases: "{{c1::Decide and inform me / Decide and consult me first / Bring me your recommendation and we'll decide together.}}"
explanation: These three phrases map to three levels of delegation (based on the Management By Objectives framework). Using them explicitly removes ambiguity and reduces unnecessary check-ins. Most managers default to 'handle it' without specifying — which leads to either micro-management or complete disconnection.
usage:
• Level 1: "Decide and inform me — I trust your judgment on this vendor."
  • Level 2: "Consult me before you finalize — I want to weigh in on the pricing structure."
  • Level 3: "Bring me your top two options with pros and cons — we'll make the call together Thursday."
spanish: Para fijar el nivel de autonomía al delegar: 'Decide e infórmame / Decide y consúltame primero / Tráeme tu recomendación y decidimos juntos.' Estos tres niveles eliminan la ambigüedad en la delegación.

text: To hold a team member accountable without being aggressive, use: "{{c1::We agreed that X would be done by [date]. It hasn't happened. What's getting in the way?}}"
explanation: This three-part accountability structure is powerful: (1) 'We agreed' — grounds the conversation in a shared commitment, not your preference; (2) 'It hasn't happened' — factual, not judgmental; (3) 'What's getting in the way?' — opens problem-solving dialogue instead of blame. It follows the SBI (Situation-Behavior-Impact) feedback model.
usage:
• "We agreed the incident report would be published by Monday. It's Thursday. What's getting in the way?"
  • "You committed to closing out those 5 tickets before the sprint ended. Three are still open. Help me understand what happened."
spanish: 'Acordamos que X estaría listo para [fecha]. No ha ocurrido. ¿Qué lo está obstaculizando?' — responsabilidad sin agresión, basada en compromisos compartidos.

text: RACI stands for {{c1::Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed}} — a framework for clarifying who does what in a project.
explanation: RACI is the most widely used responsibility assignment matrix. Key distinction: the Responsible person does the work; the Accountable person owns the outcome (one person only). Consulted means two-way input before decisions; Informed means one-way notification after. Ambiguity between R and A causes most project ownership failures.
usage:
• "Let's RACI this before we start: Sarah, you're Responsible for the build. I'm Accountable for the outcome. Alex and Paulo, you're Consulted during design. The rest of the team gets Informed at each milestone."
  • Common mistake: Multiple people listed as Accountable — always one owner per deliverable.
spanish: RACI: Responsible (hace el trabajo), Accountable (dueño del resultado — solo uno), Consulted (aporta antes de decidir), Informed (recibe notificación después). Fundamental para claridad en proyectos.

scenario: Executive English 🎯: Closing the Loop After Delegation
text: After delegating, close the loop by scheduling: {{c1::a brief check-in mid-way through, not at the deadline}}.
explanation: The most common delegation failure is waiting until the deadline to discover a problem. A mid-point check-in — framed as support, not surveillance — gives the delegate time to course-correct. Good language: 'Let's do a 10-minute check-in on Wednesday so I can remove any blockers for you.' This positions the leader as an enabler.
usage:
• "I'll delegate this fully to you. Let's do a quick sync on Tuesday mid-week so I can unblock anything. If you need me before then, ping me."
  • "You have full ownership. I'm here if you hit walls. Want to do a brief check-in Thursday, or are you good to run with it?"
spanish: Después de delegar, programa un check-in a mitad del camino, no en la fecha límite. Esto da tiempo para corregir el rumbo. Encuadra el check-in como apoyo, no como supervisión.

text: High-ownership language uses 'I will' and 'I own', while low-ownership language uses {{c1::'someone should', 'it needs to happen', or passive constructions}}.
explanation: The language of ownership is the fastest way to assess a person's leadership readiness. 'Someone should handle the escalation' diffuses accountability. 'I'll own the escalation and have a resolution plan to you by EOD' concentrates it. Training yourself to use first-person ownership language is a visible, immediate signal of leadership maturity.
usage:
• High ownership: "I'll make sure this gets resolved. You'll have an update by 3 PM."
  • Low ownership: "The team is working on it." / "It should be resolved soon."
  • Shift: Replace 'we need to look into this' with 'I'll investigate this and get back to you by [time].'
spanish: El lenguaje de alta propiedad usa 'yo haré' y 'yo dueño de', mientras que el lenguaje de baja propiedad usa 'alguien debería', 'hay que hacerlo' o construcciones pasivas. Señal visible de madurez en liderazgo.

scenario: Executive English 🎯: Giving Autonomy to Senior IC
text: When delegating to a senior individual contributor, signal trust with: "{{c1::I'm handing this over to you fully — I trust your judgment. Keep me posted on major pivots.}}"
explanation: Senior ICs and principal engineers disengage when they're micromanaged. This phrase explicitly hands over ownership, signals trust, and gives a minimal feedback expectation ('major pivots' — not every decision). It respects their expertise while keeping the leader informed on strategic changes.
usage:
• "The architecture decision is yours. I trust your read on the tradeoffs. Let me know if the direction changes significantly."
  • "You know this system better than anyone. I'm not going to second-guess your implementation choices. Just flag me if something affects our SLAs."
spanish: 'Te lo dejo completamente — confío en tu criterio. Mantenme al tanto de los cambios importantes.' Fundamental para retener y motivar a IC sénior sin microgestión.

scenario: Executive English 🎯: Recovering a Missed Commitment
text: When a team member misses a committed deadline, the recovery conversation opens with: {{c1::an acknowledgment of the impact, not a blame statement}}.
explanation: Opening with impact ('this put us behind on the client delivery') rather than blame ('you failed to deliver') keeps the conversation focused on forward solutions. Psychologically, blame activates defensiveness; impact activates accountability. Use the COIN model: Context, Observation, Impact, Next step.
usage:
• "The report being 2 days late meant we couldn't share it in the QBR. I want to understand what happened and figure out how we prevent this next time."
  • "This is not about assigning blame. I need to understand the gap so I can support you better and so we don't miss this again."
spanish: Al hablar de un compromiso incumplido, abre con el impacto, no con el reproche. El impacto activa la responsabilidad; el reproche activa la defensividad. Modelo COIN: Contexto, Observación, Impacto, Siguiente paso.
